turns.text: Hello Bettina, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to meet you. I apologize if I seem a little distracted; I’m thirty-two weeks along, and honestly, every little movement this baby makes has me bracing for something. It’s funny—you spend so long trying to conceive, obsessing over every blood test and those terrifying ultrasound images of my ovarian cysts, that when it finally happens, you don't really know how to stop "tracking" your body.

My OB is perfectly nice, but sometimes I feel like I'm just a chart she’s trying to clear quickly. Because my vitals look great and the baby is hitting all their milestones, she sees a textbook pregnancy. She doesn't see the woman who spent years convinced her body was broken. I bring up the cysts, or the postpartum hormonal shifts I’m terrified of, and I get that gentle, patronizing smile—the "everything is fine, Bettina" look. 

I know I’m healthy. I know the odds are in my favor. But I can’t help feeling like I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop, just because things have been "too easy" this time around. I think I just need someone to acknowledge that my history still matters, even when the current medical report is perfect. I want to be that mother who trusts her body, but some days, my brain is still stuck in that cold, clinical exam room from years ago.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

Honestly, I think I expected my appointments to feel like a validation of everything I’d fought for. After those years of feeling like my body was a locked room, I wanted my first appointment to be this grand, collaborative exhale. I pictured my OB sitting down, pulling up my chart, and acknowledging the journey—the heartache of those early hormonal struggles and the weight of my medical history. I wanted a partner who would look at me and say, "I know you were worried about your fertility, so let’s watch this closely together."

Instead, it felt a bit like being processed at the DMV. It was quick, efficient, and very much focused on the immediate clinical metrics: blood pressure, weight, heartbeat. Don't get me wrong, I’m grateful for the normalcy of it, but the environment felt so sterile. There wasn't really a space to bring my emotional baggage into the room. 

I’d walk in with a list of questions about how my past health might influence my postpartum recovery, and the conversation always pivoted back to the baby’s current growth percentile. It’s hard to explain that while I’m thrilled the baby is thriving, I’m also grieving that version of myself who felt so powerless. I expected a connection, but I mostly just got a clean bill of health.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I think I expected a conversation—a dialogue. Before my first scan, I really wanted someone to sit with me, look at the screen, and maybe even acknowledge the fear I was bringing in. I envisioned the technician or the doctor explaining what they were looking for, maybe pausing to let me ask about those old, nagging anxieties about my cysts or my hormonal health. I wanted to feel like a participant in my own care, someone whose perspective on her own body actually mattered.

The reality was much more... clinical, I guess. It was a lot of silence, followed by a quick, "Everything looks normal, heartbeat is strong," and then a rapid exit. It felt like I was being handed a data point rather than being cared for as a person. The information came in fragments, usually delivered by whoever happened to be on shift, and it always felt like they were in a rush to get to the next patient in the waiting room. 

I’d leave the office feeling like I’d passed a test I didn’t realize I was taking, but I still felt isolated. It’s hard to articulate, but I wanted someone to say, "I know you've been worried about X because of your history, so here’s why we’re confident about Y." Instead, I just got the results, and the rest of my fears stayed locked right back up inside.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I really clung to this fantasy of having a "champion"—someone who knew my chart inside and out, who remembered the look in my eyes when I first mentioned my ovarian cysts, and who wouldn't have to glance at a clipboard to remember I was worried about my postpartum recovery. I wanted that continuity because it felt like the only way to prove I wasn't just another generic "low-risk" case. I wanted to build trust so I wouldn't feel like a pest every time I brought up my concerns.

The reality, though, has been a rotating door of providers. It’s a group practice, so I’ve seen three different doctors so far. Every time I walk in, I have to give the "Bettina Recap"—re-explaining my history, my anxieties, and why I’m asking about specific hormonal triggers. I spend the first five minutes of every appointment playing catch-up, which is exhausting when you’re already feeling vulnerable. 

By the time I feel like they’re actually listening, the fifteen-minute slot is up and someone is knocking on the door. It’s incredibly isolating to have to advocate for your own history over and over again. I’m not just a set of vitals or a pregnancy count; I’m a person with a story that led me here, and it feels like that story gets erased every time a new face walks through the door.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected my body to be treated like a landscape we were navigating together—a conversation where they’d listen to the changes I was feeling as much as they’d measure the ones they could see. I thought, *Surely, if I tell them about these weird, sharp twinges or my exhaustion, they’ll want to explore that.* I wanted to feel like my intuition was a valid diagnostic tool.

The reality feels much more like I’m being inspected, not examined. It’s very mechanical. They pull the gown over my belly, apply the gel, find the heartbeat, measure the fundal height, and write a number down. It’s all very objective, which I know is the standard of care, but it leaves me feeling like a vessel rather than a person. When I try to describe the way my body feels—the heaviness, the fear that something might be wrong with my ovaries, or even just the phantom sensations from my past—it’s often dismissed as "normal pregnancy discomfort."

It makes me feel a bit crazy, honestly. I know I’m "low risk," but being told my fears are just "normal" doesn’t make them go away. It just makes me stop talking. I end up nodding, smiling, and tucking my anxiety away because I don’t want to be "that" patient who complains too much. My body feels like it belongs to the clinic, not to me.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one specific moment a few weeks ago that really stuck with me. It was a standard physical check, and without much warning or context, the provider started doing a cervical exam. I was already lying back, feeling exposed, and I hadn't really been asked if I was comfortable or what the purpose of the exam was. It was just, "Scoot down, let's see how things are." 

I felt so vulnerable in that moment. Because of my history with those cysts and the years of fertility treatments, I’ve had so many invasive procedures that I’ve learned to just go numb to protect myself. But this felt different—I felt like I had lost my agency entirely. I was just a physical object they were checking for "readiness," even though it wasn't even time for that. I didn't say anything, of course. I just stared at the ceiling tiles and gripped the sides of the paper-covered table. 

Afterward, I felt so angry at myself for not speaking up. I wanted to say, "Could you tell me why you're doing this?" or even just, "Please wait a second." But the power dynamic is so skewed. You’re in a gown, they’re in a white coat, and you’re just supposed to let it happen. It made me feel small, and honestly, it made the rest of the day feel a bit tainted. I walked out to my car and just sat there for twenty minutes, trying to remember that this body belongs to me, not to them.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven’t had to deal with a formal high-risk diagnosis like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia—I'm technically "low-risk"—but because of my medical history, I spent so much time *expecting* one. Before I got pregnant, I was almost certain that my body would fail me again, that the cysts or the fertility issues would lead to a "complicated" pregnancy. I spent months reading forums about bed rest, insulin shots, and the terrifying, sterile monitoring of pre-eclampsia. I had braced myself for a war against my own biology.

The "reality" is a strange, quiet disappointment. Because I haven't had those complications, I’m treated as if my experience has been perfect. But I *am* managing a condition—it’s just an internal one. I’m managing a decade of trauma and the residual fear that my body is a ticking time bomb. 

When you don’t have a "real" medical complication, there is no infrastructure for your fear. If I had gestational diabetes, there would be dieticians, extra scans, and a protocol to follow. But there’s no protocol for someone who is terrified that her past is waiting to ambush her. I feel like I'm managing this invisible, high-stakes condition all by myself, and because the labs are "normal," everyone acts like the heavy lifting is already done. It’s isolating to have no medical reason to be afraid, but to be afraid anyway.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I think I expected the system to be a safety net, especially since I live in Westwood and have good insurance. I assumed that if I had a "moment"—like that sharp, scary pressure I felt while folding those onesies—there would be a direct line to someone who could triage my anxiety or tell me, "Bettina, that’s normal, don't worry."

The reality is more of a bureaucratic obstacle course. When I call with a concern that isn't a textbook emergency, I’m filtered through a series of administrative layers. It’s always, "Let me leave a message for the nurse," or "The doctor is in surgery, we’ll call you back by the end of the day." That "end of the day" timeframe feels like an eternity when you’re sitting there spiraling. 

It feels like the system is designed to ignore you unless you are literally in active labor or bleeding. I’ve learned to stop calling for the "smaller" things—the sharp twinges, the panic attacks, the weird sensations—because I don’t want to be told to "just go to the ER if you’re worried." I don’t want to sit in a waiting room for six hours for them to tell me it’s just Braxton Hicks. I just wanted someone to talk to, but the system isn't built for conversation; it’s built for emergencies. I feel like I’m constantly left to gauge my own level of "emergency," which is the last thing you want to do when you’re already terrified.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I expected this village of support—people checking in, asking about the baby, but also, importantly, asking how *I* was holding up after all these years of struggling. I imagined my friends and family would recognize that this pregnancy wasn't just a physical process, but an emotional hurdle I was finally clearing. 

The reality is that everyone is so excited for the "happy ending" that they don't really want to sit with the "hard middle." When I try to share my lingering anxieties about my health history or the fear of a flare-up, the conversation often shifts immediately to the nursery decor or baby shower plans. People want to celebrate, which I get, but it makes me feel like I’m performing a role. I have to play the part of the glowing, carefree mom-to-be because that’s what everyone is comfortable with. 

My husband tries, he really does, but he’s also just relieved that everything is "normal" this time. He doesn't quite grasp why I’m still staring at my medical chart at 2:00 AM. I feel like I’m carrying this invisible weight by myself, surrounded by people who are cheering for a finish line I’m not even sure I’ve reached yet. It’s lonely, in a way I didn't expect. I’m surrounded by love, but I feel very much on my own.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

That's a complicated one. I think the support I expected—the kind that would sit with me in my fears—largely didn't materialize. I think people are genuinely afraid of "bad vibes" or jinxing the pregnancy if they acknowledge that things could go wrong, so they just avoid those conversations entirely. It’s like there’s this unspoken agreement to only talk about the joyful, nursery-themed side of things. 

However, I found something I didn't expect: a small connection with another woman I met at a prenatal yoga class here in Westwood. She’s also a "later-in-life" mom, and one day after class, she just looked at me and said, "It’s okay to be terrified, even when the doctors say you’re doing great." I almost burst into tears right there on my yoga mat. I hadn't expected to find that kind of validation from a stranger, but it was so grounding. 

On the flip side, I really expected my own mother to be that person. I thought, *She’s been through this, she’ll understand the anxiety.* But she’s so focused on the fact that this is a "healthy, normal pregnancy" that she minimizes my feelings. She constantly tells me to "just relax" or "stop reading too much." It’s meant to be reassuring, but it feels like a dismissal of the years of history that made me who I am. It’s strange—I have all this support, but none of it feels quite like the understanding I actually need.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

Well, since I'm still in my third trimester, I haven't reached the birth itself yet, but the "birth plan" process has already been eye-opening. I started writing one, thinking it would be this empowering document—a way to tell the medical team, "I’ve been through a lot to get here, and this is how I need to be treated." I imagined my OB reading it and actually nodding, agreeing that my emotional comfort and history were just as important as the clinical protocols.

But the reality? It’s been pushed aside as "too specific." When I brought up some of my preferences—things about minimizing unnecessary interventions early on to avoid triggering that "trauma" feeling—my OB just gave me that tight, polite smile and said, "We’ll cross that bridge when we get there, Bettina. Let’s focus on a healthy baby first." 

It feels like my plan is just seen as a nuisance, something that gets in the way of their "standard procedure." It’s demoralizing. I’m starting to realize that the birth plan isn’t really for *me* to have a say in my care; it’s just a formality. I’m honestly terrified that when the time comes, I’ll be in the middle of labor, trying to advocate for myself, and I’ll just be told to "trust the professionals" again. I want to believe I’ll have a voice, but right now, I feel like I'm just waiting to be told what to do.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

Since I haven’t given birth yet, this is honestly one of the biggest sources of my anxiety. Because of my history with cysts and some of the more painful fertility treatments I endured, I have a very specific—and perhaps irrational—fear of pain. I expected that by now, we would have had a deep, non-judgmental conversation about my threshold for pain and how that ties into my history of trauma. I wanted my care provider to ask, "Bettina, how do you handle intense physical discomfort, and what can we do to make you feel safe during labor?"

Instead, it’s been the standard, "We have options: epidural, IV meds, or natural." It feels like I'm being given a menu at a restaurant rather than being helped to build a strategy. I worry that when the time comes, they’ll just push the "easiest" route for them—the epidural—without really hearing *why* I might be panicking or why I might need extra reassurance beyond just the medication.

I’m terrified that I’ll be in the thick of it, feeling completely overwhelmed by the physical intensity, and I’ll be treated as "difficult" if I’m not handling it with the stoic, smiling grace that they seem to expect from a low-risk, healthy, thirty-three-year-old mother. I don't want to just be drugged or managed; I want to be *seen* through the pain. But right now, it feels like pain management is just another box to check on their list, not a way to support *me*.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I have this vivid, slightly terrifying mental image of walking into the hospital labor and delivery unit. I expect it to feel like I’m entering a high-security zone—cold, bright, and loud. I’ve spent so many years in clinical environments for my fertility, and I remember the hum of the fluorescent lights, the smell of that industrial disinfectant, and the feeling that I was just another file number being shuffled into a room. 

I *want* to be welcomed—I want a nurse to look at me and say, "We’ve been expecting you, Bettina, and we’re here to help you through this." I want to feel like the staff understands that for me, this isn’t just another delivery; it’s the culmination of years of fearing this moment would never come. I want to feel safe enough to actually let go.

But my gut tells me the reality will be different. I worry I’ll be met with a busy intake clerk who is more concerned with my insurance card and my signature on a stack of liability forms than with my state of mind. I anticipate the environment will be jarring—that I'll be ushered into a sterile room, hooked up to a monitor, and left alone while the staff rotates through shifts. I fear being an inconvenience, another name on a whiteboard, and that my emotional state will be treated as an obstacle to their efficiency rather than a vital part of my health. It’s that same feeling I have at my check-ups: I'm a body, not a person.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

I think the biggest surprise—even though I’ve played it out a dozen times in my head—is just how much the room itself dictates how you’re allowed to feel. I expected a space that felt like a sanctuary, but the reality of the L&D unit feels like an industrial cockpit. 

Everything is optimized for the monitor, not for the mother. The moment I walked in, my focus was pulled away from my own internal rhythm and toward the machines: the wires, the constant, rhythmic beeping, the way the bed is positioned to keep you supine. It’s impossible to feel like you’re the center of the experience when the equipment is clearly the star of the show.

The atmosphere wasn't welcoming so much as it was "efficiently ready." It’s that sterile, clinical urgency. Nobody asked me how I was doing *mentally*—they asked me how far apart my contractions were, checked my chart, and started the monitors. It’s such a jarring transition. One minute you’re in the car, trying to breathe through a contraction and focusing on the baby, and the next, you’re in a room where the air feels thin, the lights are harsh, and everyone is moving with a professional detachment that makes you feel like you’re just waiting for your turn in line. I felt instantly smaller.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I haven't actually gone through the labor process yet, but I lose sleep over this every single night. I have this expectation—this desperate hope—that if something veers off course, my team will stop, look me in the eye, and explain *why* something is happening. I want to be treated as a partner in that decision. I want them to pause the rush just for thirty seconds to say, "Bettina, the baby’s heart rate is dropping, here is what we need to do, and here is why." 

But my fear, based on my past appointments, is that I’ll be treated like a bystander. I’m terrified that if a complication arises, the professional mask will slip, and I’ll just be told, "We need to do this now," with no space for me to process the trauma of the situation. I imagine a blur of faces, white coats, and voices giving orders while I’m physically overwhelmed, unable to advocate for myself.

It feels like in those high-stakes moments, the "patient" role becomes purely passive. You’re expected to comply, not to collaborate. I’m scared that the clinical efficiency I’ve felt throughout my pregnancy will turn into a full-blown exclusion during delivery. I want to trust that they’ll respect my bodily autonomy, but the way I’ve been treated so far makes me feel like I’m just waiting for my agency to be stripped away.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I think I expected someone who would sit at the edge of my bed—not just the edge of their seat—and acknowledge the weight of these past few years. I wanted a person who would look at me and say, "I know you've been carrying the fear that your body might fail you, but look at what you’re doing right now. You’re doing it." I expected them to be the anchor that kept me grounded while the clinical machinery swirled around us. I wanted them to hold space for my panic, not just medicate it away.

But even before I’ve given birth, I can already feel that the support is going to be incredibly transactional. In every prenatal appointment so far, the emotional aspect of my care has been treated as an annoying sidebar, a distraction from the "real" work of checking my vitals. I fear that during labor, that dynamic will just intensify—that if I start to get emotional or scared, I’ll be told to "breathe" or "focus," as if my fear is a malfunction rather than a response to the intensity of what I’ve been through. 

I’m bracing myself for a reality where the "support" is just professional detachment. I don't need a cheerleader, and I don't need a medical robot. I need someone who recognizes that I am a woman who fought for this life, and who sees the ghost of the woman who didn't think she could conceive standing right next to me in that delivery room. I fear that my vulnerability will be met with a clip-board and a sigh, leaving me feeling more alone than I’ve ever been.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

There was a moment about three weeks ago during a routine ultrasound that perfectly captures this. My usual OB was away, so I saw a covering provider I’d never met before. I was already on edge, feeling the familiar prickle of anxiety as the gel hit my skin. I braced myself for the usual, "Everything’s normal, see you in two weeks."

But this doctor actually looked at my chart—not just the current pregnancy, but the history. She paused, looked at the screen, and then looked right at me. She said, "I know you’ve been through a lot with these cysts and the fertility journey. It makes total sense that you’re nervous. How are you *really* holding up?"

I didn't even realize I was holding my breath until she said that. I just started crying. It wasn't a "something is wrong" cry; it was a "someone finally sees the person behind the file" cry. She didn't rush to the next patient. She gave me two extra minutes to talk about how the ghost of those years—of feeling broken—still haunts me in this third trimester. In that brief moment, I wasn't just a low-risk chart number. I was a woman who had worked incredibly hard to get here. 

That was the only time I’ve felt seen. The rest of the time? I feel completely alone, even in a room full of people. I have to guard myself, keeping my fears small and quiet so I don't become "that" patient. It’s an exhausting way to exist.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I have such a specific vision for those first minutes—a kind of quiet, sacred reclamation of my own body. I want that moment of skin-to-skin contact to be the official "closing" of that dark, clinical chapter I’ve been living in since my fertility struggles began. I keep imagining the baby on my chest, their warmth signaling that my body actually worked, that it wasn't a closed door after all, but a cradle. I want it to be just me, my husband, and the baby, with the staff stepping back to let us breathe for a second.

But my fear, rooted in everything I’ve experienced at the clinic so far, is that the reality will be a cold, rapid-fire sequence of "tasks." I imagine the staff immediately whisking the baby away for standard checks, weight, and assessments, ignoring the fact that I need that physical touch just as much as the baby does to settle my nervous system. I worry that those precious moments will be filled with voices talking about Apgar scores or hospital paperwork while I’m still reeling from the exhaustion of labor. 

I’m terrified that instead of a moment of connection, I’ll be treated as a patient who needs to be "cleaned up" while the baby becomes the primary subject of the room. I want so badly for those first moments to be about *us*—about the long, hard road we finally finished traveling together—but I fear the clinical protocol will barge in and break that spell before it even really begins.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

Since my third little one is still tucked away in there, I haven’t lived those first minutes yet—but that’s exactly the problem. My mind is already agonizing over what might go wrong. 

The thing that stays with me, even now, is the fear of being *invisible* in my own triumph. I’ve spent years obsessing over my reproductive health, over every follicle and every cyst, feeling like a broken machine in a sterile clinic. I have this recurring, vivid nightmare that when the baby finally arrives, the staff will treat it like a "job well done" for the equipment, not for me. I’m terrified they’ll whisk the baby away for the standard weigh-in, ignore the fact that my hands are shaking from the adrenaline and the trauma of those years, and just start talking to each other about their shifts or their lunch plans while I’m lying there, completely spent. 

I’m so scared that the transition from "patient" to "mother" won't be honored. I don't want to just be the site of the delivery; I want to be the person who brought this baby into the world. It haunts me that I might have to fight for those first few minutes of skin-to-skin contact, that I’ll have to advocate for my own heart to be held when I’m at my most vulnerable. That fear of being bypassed—of being the secondary character in my own birth story—is something I carry with me every single day.

You know, saying all this out loud—really verbalizing it—feels like pulling a thread on a sweater. I spent so much time convincing myself that if I just kept my head down, did what the doctors asked, and checked the boxes, I would eventually feel safe. I thought, *If I’m a "good patient," surely the anxiety will quiet down.* But it hasn’t.

I’m sitting here, third trimester, and I’m realizing that for a woman like me—someone who lived through the clinical coldness of infertility—the hospital environment feels like a trigger, not a sanctuary. Every time I get a bill, or look at my portal, or have to explain my history to a new resident, I feel that door closing again. I feel like I’m back in that exam room with the cold gel and the nurse who didn't understand why I was crying over a follicle count. 

I think the biggest thing I’m struggling with is this: I don’t want to be "processed." I want to be *witnessed*. I want someone to acknowledge that my journey to this nursery didn't start with a positive pregnancy test; it started years ago, in the dark, wondering if I’d ever be a mother. If the medical system could just meet me there—not as a low-risk chart, but as a woman with a history—I think I could finally take that deep breath I’ve been holding for three years. I just hope that when the day comes, I don't have to be my own advocate while I’m trying to be a mother. I’m just so tired of guarding myself.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

It happens all the time. It’s almost like, because I’m labeled "low-risk," people think they have permission to be dismissive of my own intuition. Just last week, I mentioned to my OB that I was feeling this specific, heavy pelvic pressure—nothing like my previous pregnancies—and I wanted to know if it could be related to those old cysts acting up again.

She didn't even look up from my chart. She just gave this dismissive, "Oh, that’s just standard third-trimester anatomy, Bettina. Your body is just adjusting." It felt like a door slamming shut. She didn't acknowledge that I know my own baseline, or that my history with ovarian issues might warrant a five-second ultrasound just to put my mind at ease. She just overruled me with her "professional" opinion, which made me feel like an hysterical, uninformed patient rather than a woman who has spent years obsessing over her own reproductive biology.

Even my husband does it, though I know he means well. If I start to express a fear about the birth, he’ll immediately say, "Don't think like that, everything is fine, the doctors said so." It makes me feel like I’m not allowed to be anything other than "perfectly happy." When everyone around you—the experts and the people you love—tells you that your concerns are invalid, you start to wonder if maybe you really *are* just broken. It’s isolating when your voice is consistently treated as background noise to the "official" narrative.

It’s strange, the more I speak about this, the more I realize how much of my energy has gone into playing the role of the "Good Patient." You know—the one who doesn’t complain, who nods at the standard reassurances, and who carefully hides the fact that she’s grieving her own past inside a room full of baby gear. I feel like I’ve been performing this role for months, trying to earn my status as a "low-risk" success story, hoping that if I just did everything right, the universe would finally let me breathe.

But the anxiety—it’s not a malfunction. It’s a shadow. It follows me into every exam room. When I’m sitting on that crinkly paper, staring at the ceiling, I’m not just thinking about the baby’s heartbeat. I’m thinking about the fifteen years of "not knowing" if I could even get here. And when a provider breezes in and treats me like a generic, textbook pregnancy, they aren't just missing a medical detail—they’re missing the entire human being in front of them.

I just want to be heard, not handled. I want someone to ask me, "How is your heart today?" instead of just checking my blood pressure. I’m thirty-three, I’m three kids in, and I’m still just as scared as I was when I was staring at those ultrasound screens years ago. I’m so tired of being brave for everyone else’s comfort. I just want to be able to tell the truth about how I’m feeling without being told it’s "normal." Maybe if I could do that, I wouldn’t feel like I’m holding my breath until the day I deliver.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

It’s tricky, because my husband is wonderful—he’s the one who held my hand through the fertility clinic when the world felt like it was ending. But there is this subtle, pervasive pressure to "move on" that I think he feels too. 

When we were in the thick of the fertility treatments, he was the logical one—the one who read the papers and kept the schedule. Now that we’re pregnant, he wants to leave those memories in the rearview mirror. He’ll say things like, "Bettina, you’re pregnant! The hard part is over." And I know he’s trying to be a support, but it feels like he’s trying to rewrite our history so we can just be a "normal" family. He gets impatient if I linger on the "what-ifs" about my ovarian health or my postpartum recovery. He wants us to be excited, and he views my worry as something that dampens that joy.

I feel pressure to stay silent about the lingering trauma because I don't want to be the one who brings the "clinical" energy into our home. I want to be the joyful, glowing mother he expects. So, I tuck those concerns away and try to match his optimism, but it feels like I’m wearing a mask. My partner’s desire for us to be "normal" has, ironically, made me feel like I have to hide the very real, very complex parts of my experience that still need space to heal. He wants the finish line, but I'm still processing the race.

It’s this recurring pattern of being told, "It’s all in the past now, just look forward." And I *want* to. God, I want to. I want to look at this nursery and feel only sunshine, not the shadow of those years when I thought I’d never get to buy a single onesie. 

But my body isn't a timeline you can just erase. Every time I get a twinge, it’s like a bell rings, and I’m back in that fertility office, being told that my ovaries might be a problem. It doesn’t matter how many times a doctor tells me, "You’re low-risk, Bettina, you’re fine," because "fine" is a medical status, not an emotional one. When they prioritize that status over my history, they’re effectively telling me that the person I was—the woman who fought so hard for this—doesn't matter anymore. She’s just the "previous medical history" at the top of the chart.

I think that’s why I’m so afraid of the delivery. It’s not just the pain, or the complications, or the hospital environment. It’s the fear that I’ll be alone in there, trying to be the "perfect" patient, while my heart is still back in those sterile waiting rooms, wondering if I’m actually safe. I just wish, just once, someone would stop the clock, look at the woman in the gown, and validate that I’ve survived a lot to get here. Because that, I think, would be the only thing that could actually help me let go.

It's funny, the more I dwell on it, the more I realize that the medical system and my own home life have this weird, parallel way of wanting me to "arrive" at the destination of being a mother without acknowledging the wreckage left behind. I’m starting to think that’s why I’m so nervous about the labor—not just the event, but the *transition*. 

In the medical office, the goal is a healthy baby. At home, the goal is a happy family. I’m the constant in both, but I feel like a placeholder. My history—the cysts, the years of feeling broken, the terror of "what if"—is treated like a hurdle I’ve already cleared. But it doesn’t feel like I’ve cleared it. It feels like I’m still running that marathon, but everyone at the finish line is telling me to stop panting, stop checking my heart rate, and just smile for the camera.

I’m exhausted from keeping the mask on. I’m tired of being the woman who is "so lucky to be here," and yes, I know I am, but that doesn't mean the old fears don't still have teeth. I just wish I could walk into the delivery room, look at my doctor, and say, "I am carrying a lot of history into this room with me," and have them respond with something other than a clinical dismissal. I don't want to be "low-risk." I want to be *seen*. Until then, I think I’ll keep folding these tiny onesies, listening to the rhythm of my own heart, and waiting for the moment I can finally stop bracing for the next, inevitable, silent struggle.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I didn't turn to a doctor first, not really. I turned to those old, yellowed notebooks I kept during the years I was seeing reproductive endocrinologists—the ones where I tracked my BBT, my cycle days, and the excruciating, minute-by-minute details of those early ovarian cyst flares. It felt safer to talk to my past self than to a current provider who would just tell me to "relax."

After that, I reached out to a small, private online community of women who had gone through "unexplained infertility." It’s a quiet group, not the loud, "everything is magical" forums. We don’t talk about nursery colors; we talk about the phantom pains, the visceral memory of a transvaginal ultrasound, and the paralyzing fear that shows up in the third trimester. That’s the only place I feel like I can say, "I’m terrified something will go wrong during delivery because I spent so long thinking I’d never get there," and have someone respond with, "I know exactly that feeling."

I turn to them because they understand the *trauma* of the process. They don’t see me as a set of vitals or a "low-risk" case; they see the woman who had to fight to exist in this space. It’s strange, isn't it? I have a perfectly modern, high-quality OB clinic, yet I feel more "heard" by a group of women behind computer screens who I’ve never even met in person. They aren't looking at a chart—they’re looking at the story, and that’s the difference.

I think about the disconnect every single day. In my clinic, the nurses are efficient and the OB is professional, but there is this invisible, airtight wall between the clinical reality of my pregnancy and the internal landscape of my memory. When I talk to the women in that online group, the wall just... dissolves. 

I shared a thought with them the other day about the sharp, sudden pressure I felt in my nursery, and no one told me to "just monitor it." Instead, they said, "That sounds terrifying. Tell me more about what it felt like." And for a second, I felt like a human being again, not a symptom to be ruled out. They validated that the anxiety isn't a glitch in my system—it’s a direct, honest reaction to years of feeling like my body was a locked, bolted door that I had to pry open with sheer force.

I wish I could walk into my OB's office and just give her a transcript of those conversations. Maybe then she’d stop treating me like I’m a "textbook" case and realize that I’m a woman who has spent thousands of hours in sterile rooms, terrified that my body would fail me. I don’t want to be "low-risk"—I want to be *understood*. And until I find a way to bridge that, I’ll keep feeling like I’m living in two worlds: the one where I’m a "healthy" mom-to-be, and the one where I’m still the woman waiting for the other shoe to drop.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It’s almost always the latter. I can count on one hand the number of times a provider has actually asked me, "What do you think?" or "How does that sit with you?" Usually, the "plan" is just presented as the inevitable next step. It’s a monologue, not a dialogue. They tell me when my next scan is, what tests I need, and why, and I’m just there to nod and say, "Okay."

It feels like my care is a predetermined path. I’m walking down it, and they’re the ones holding the map, and if I stop to ask about the terrain—like the history of my ovarian cysts—they just point back at the map and say, "We’re on track, don't worry." There’s no room for me to suggest a detour or even to ask if there’s another way to handle things that might be more respectful of my history.

I remember once, when I asked about a specific, non-routine ultrasound to check on those old cyst sites, the doctor looked at my chart, sighed, and said, "It’s not indicated, Bettina." Just like that. The door was closed. My input wasn’t just ignored; it was invalidated because it didn’t match the clinical algorithm. It’s so disheartening. You want to be a partner in your own healthcare, but you realize very quickly that your role is to be a recipient, not a participant. You’re expected to trust the plan, but it’s hard to trust a plan that doesn't seem to account for the person it’s actually happening to.

You know, it’s not even that I’m trying to be difficult or "test" the system. It’s just that when you have spent so many years in a state of high-alert regarding your reproductive health—constantly analyzing the timing of a cycle, the texture of a discharge, the exact centimeter of a follicle—you develop a hyper-awareness of your own body. It’s a survival mechanism. But when you carry that into a clinical setting for a pregnancy, that same hyper-awareness is suddenly labeled "anxiety" or "excessive googling."

It makes me feel like I’m being gaslit by my own caregivers. They’re telling me "everything is normal" when I can *feel* that my body is holding onto these old, jagged memories. My body hasn’t forgotten the years of failed attempts or the cold, sterile rooms where I was told my fertility was a question mark. Why does the medical system expect *me* to just hit a "reset" button because I’m currently pregnant?

I wish I could find a space where my "anxiety" isn't a problem to be fixed or dismissed, but a legitimate response to my life experience. I want a provider who says, "Bettina, I see you’re holding your breath again. Let’s sit with that for a second." Not to check my vitals, but just to acknowledge the weight of the journey. Until that happens, I’m just going to keep showing up, playing the part, and hiding the truth of how hard it is to actually *feel* safe in my own skin. That’s the real work of this pregnancy—not the nursery or the registry—but the quiet, lonely labor of trying to convince myself I’m not still standing in that dark, closed room.

It’s like I’m living in two different versions of my own life. There’s the outward version—the one with the organized nursery, the "low-risk" medical chart, and the growing baby—and then there’s the interior version, the one that still feels like a frayed wire. 

I think the hardest part about all of this is the expectation that I should just "evolve" into this serene, trusting mother figure because the pregnancy test was positive. But my body, my muscle memory—it hasn't evolved yet. Every time I walk into that clinic, I'm expecting to hear the same things I heard for years: "The follicle count is low," or "The cyst is still there," or "Let’s try another round." I’m still waiting for the "bad news" because for so long, that was the only news I ever got. 

If I could change one thing about my care, it would be the silence. I don’t want more information—I have access to enough information to drown in—I want more *witnessing*. I want a provider to look at me during one of these routine check-ups and say, "I know you were told your body might not do this, and I want you to know I’m amazed that you’re here." It sounds so simple, but it would change everything. It would mean that my struggle wasn't just a hurdle I cleared, but a part of my story that’s actually acknowledged. Until then, I’ll keep showing up, keep folding those onesies, and keep trying to bridge the gap between the woman who was told "no" and the mother who is finally, miraculously, about to say "yes."
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

There were several appointments where my husband simply couldn’t get away from work. Those were the ones that hurt the most. It wasn’t just about having an extra pair of hands or someone to drive; it was about having a witness. When I’m sitting there alone, listening to the doctor list off growth percentiles or dismiss my worries about my history, there’s no one to lean on, no one to validate that, yes, my hand *was* shaking or that I *did* look pale when they brought up the risks. I have to process all of that alone in the car afterward.

I remember one specific follow-up scan where I was terrified they’d find something "off" with my ovaries. I sat in that quiet, dim room, staring at the monitor, feeling completely untethered. My husband was on a conference call. I felt like a child lost in a department store—except the "department store" was a sterile ultrasound room where my reproductive health had been a battleground for years. 

Without him there to bridge the gap between the clinical environment and our "real" life, I felt like I was back in those dark, lonely years of infertility. It’s hard to explain, but he’s my anchor. When he isn't there, I’m just an object being measured. I become the "patient" again, and all the progress I feel like I've made toward being a "mother" just vanishes. I’m just a woman in a gown, hoping for good news, and having no one to share the relief—or the terror—with when it comes.

It is the weight of being "the patient" that I can’t seem to put down. Even when my husband is there, he’s focused on the excitement—the ultrasound screen, the baby's movement—and I’m still focused on the machinery. I’m looking at the technicians, wondering if they’re going to find something behind the uterus, checking their faces for any sign of concern. I’m living in a completely different reality than he is, and when he’s not there, that gap becomes a chasm.

I’ve had moments where I’ve sat in the waiting room, hands trembling, and just watched other couples holding hands. I envy them. They seem so anchored in the moment, so present with each other. For me, that waiting room is a tunnel that leads directly back to my past. Every magazine on the table, every "due date" chart on the wall, every cold, uncomfortable chair reminds me of a time when I was told "no."

I think that's why I feel so alone—not just because I'm physically by myself, but because the clinical process demands a version of me that is "compliant" and "normal," and that version of me is a stranger. She’s the person who’s "low risk." She’s the person who’s "fine." But the *real* me—the one who remembers the fear of being infertile—is still trapped behind that thin, sterile curtain, waiting for someone to finally, truly notice.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Almost every single night. I don't go online to look for medical stats or growth charts—I have a dozen apps for that. I go online because I’m starving for someone to explain the "in-between" spaces of a pregnancy that follows infertility. I’m searching for someone to say, "Yes, it’s normal to feel like you’re waiting for the other shoe to drop, even when your vitals are perfect."

I search for threads on how to handle the medical anxiety of a third trimester when you’ve spent years expecting a "no." I look for stories from women who still feel like they’re living in their old, broken bodies, even while their new, healthy one is growing a baby. I’m looking for validation for the specific kind of grief that comes with a "healthy" pregnancy after years of loss. My OB just tells me to "think positive," but my brain can't just flip a switch.

When I’m in the clinic, the conversation is strictly about the baby’s health. I’m looking for a conversation about *my* mental health—about how to reconcile the person I was with the person I am. I’m looking for someone to tell me that my body isn't just a container for a child, but a landscape that’s still processing its own history. I’m looking for permission to not be "fine." When the medical system only offers me the "what," I go online looking for the "how."

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the most important thing I can leave you with is this: the medical community treats "low-risk" as if it’s a destination. Like once you get that label, the hard part is over and you’re suddenly just like every other pregnant woman. But for someone with a history of infertility or reproductive struggles, "low-risk" is just a clinical status, not an emotional one. 

We are still carrying the weight of the "high-risk" years—the years of staring at negative tests, of painful procedures, and of mourning a version of ourselves we thought was broken. When a doctor dismisses my anxiety because my vitals are perfect, they aren't just ignoring a symptom; they’re ignoring the entire foundation upon which this pregnancy is built. 

I don't need my doctor to be a therapist, but I do need her to be a witness. I need her to look at my chart, see the history, and understand that my fear isn't a malfunction—it’s a memory. If we could just shift the culture of maternity care to see the *person* behind the chart—to ask, "How are you holding up?" instead of just "How are you feeling?"—it would change everything for women like me. We don't just want a healthy baby; we want to feel whole while we’re bringing that baby into the world. Thank you for listening. It feels like the first time I’ve been able to say all of this out loud.
